God's judgment in Ezekiel 6 is not abstract or distant—it is visceral and comprehensive. The prophet is commanded to prophesy against the very landscape where Israel has turned to idolatry, declaring that mountains and valleys themselves will witness destruction. This is not simply political or military defeat. God will scatter the bones of His people around their altars, leaving corpses before the idols they worshipped. The repetition throughout this chapter—"you will know that I am the LORD"—drives home a terrible reality: Israel will learn who God is through the experience of His judgment. They have forgotten Him in the abundance of their covenant blessings, choosing instead the high places and carved images that promised power and fertility. Now they will encounter God's power unmistakably, but as the one who tears down what they built and scatters what they treasured.

Yet even in this unrelenting pronouncement of judgment, God's character does not collapse into mere wrath. Verse 8 introduces a stunning reversal: "Yet I will leave a remnant." This is not a concession God makes reluctantly. It is God's faithfulness asserting itself even through judgment. Some will survive the sword. They will be scattered into exile—humiliated, displaced, torn from their land—but they will survive. And in that exile, something crucial happens. The survivors will remember God. In their desolation among foreign nations, stripped of their idols and their high places, they will recall how they broke His heart. They will loathe themselves for the abominations they committed, not because they are forced to, but because they will see clearly what their unfaithfulness cost them.

This is the path God uses to reclaim His people: destruction that leads to remembrance, judgment that produces repentance. The exile is not the final word. It is the necessary wilderness through which God's people must pass to be restored. Notice that those who survive do not merely regret their actions—they remember God Himself, how grieved He was by their adulterous hearts. The shame they feel is inseparable from their return to relationship with Him. God's judgment is severe because His love is real and His covenant is not negotiable. He will not share His people's hearts with idols, and He will go to devastating lengths to make them His own again. When you read of bodies scattered among altars and land made desolate, remember: this is what it looks like when God refuses to abandon His people, even in judgment.
